title: 第 26 節
en: Words for Soul in Hebrew.

Let us look at some of the words for soul in Hebrew. The distinction between the body and a something that gives life to it was familiar to the Jews from the earliest times ^. Body was the flesh, ib'Zi, basar. Hence ' all flesh ' meant everybody, every living being, and 'my flesh' was used for I myself. The flesh, as active and living, was opposed on one side to what is without life, stones or bones ; on the other side, as being mortal, weak, and perishing, it was distinguished from what is eternal and imperishable, God and the soul.

The question then arises, how did the Jews call that which was not the body ? They called it by different names, and these names had at first a very vague and undefined meaning, so that they are often used one in place of the other. To attempt to define ^ See H. Schultz, Alttestamentliche Theologie, p. 622.
these words strictly is a mistake, though at a later time the Jews themselves tried to define their old vague terms.

One of these words is n^"i, riiach, which means originally what moves, either the wind or the breath. As thunder was called the voice of God, the storm was conceived as His breath. This riiach in man is life, given by God and returning to God. It is used for man's breath, but not for breath only, but likewise for what we mean by soul, for we read of the riiach being embittered, cast down, and grieved, or being revived and glad.

Another word, HD^J, neshamah, has nearly the same meaning. It is used for the vital breath which every creature has received from God, and likewise for the creature that breathes and lives. Thus, when we read in Genesis ii. 7, that ' the Lord God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life : and man became a living soul,' the word neshamah is clearly conceived as breath coming from God, and as giving not only life, but also what we mean by soul, the latter being impossible without the former.

A third word also, K'sj, nephesh, does not seem from the beginning to have had a very different meaning. But it soon came to be used for the individual soul rather than for life and spirit in general. Nephesh is the living soul, the self-conscious soul. Hence it is the nephesh that loves and hates, that is glad and sorrowful, and ' my nephesh ' is a very common expression for I myself. We meet with such expressions as ' my soul lives,' ' my soul dies,' and even the dead is still called a nephesh. But to say in every passage where THE DISCOVERY OF THE SOUL. 199 nephesh occurs, whether it was meant for breath or life or soul is impossible. When Elijah prayed over the dead child, he said : ' O Lord my God, I pray thee, let this child's soul come into his inward parts again.' 'And the Lord heard the voice of Elijah, and the soul of the child came unto him again, and he revived.' Here (1 Kings xvii. 21) the soul may be meant for mere breath, the actual breath, just as we say that a drowned person has been reanimated by breathing into his lungs. But it may also mean the soul as given by God and returning to God at the time of death .

We saw that this soul is sometimes said to be in the blood, or to be the blood. And like the blood, the heart also, leb, is conceived as the seat of the soul, or is used synonymously with soul. Not only feelings and passions, but the conscience also, nay reason and understanding, dwell in the heart. Hence we read of a pure heart, of a new heart. We read (Hos. vii. 11) of Ephraim being 'like a silly dove without heart,' that is, without understanding.

We see here the same wealth of expression which we generally meet with in ancient languages. The same thing is called by different names, according to some characteristic mark that strikes the speaker. These names, being at first almost synonymous, become gradually differentiated by usage, till the time comes when they are defined more or less arbitrarily by grammarians, philosophers, or theologians ^.

^ In later times the Jewish Eabbis defined the different names of soul more accurately, but also more arbitrarily. They tell us (Midrash Genes. Eabba, cap. 14 ad fin,") that there are in man five spiritual potentiae, each having its own name. They are (1) ^^2, the vital power ; (2) rVD, the vital spirit ; (3) r\12^2, conscious- 200 LECTUKE VII.

What is important for us to observe is the fact that the original meaning of all these words was material. The idea of life was borrowed from blood, from heart, from breath, and that idea included in time not onlyphysical life, but feeling, sensuous perception, and understanding, nay soul itself, as a self-conscious and personal, if not immortal, being. There are other expressions in Hebrew which start from even more material beginnings, and end by meaning the soul or the self. Thus when the Psalmist (vi. 3 seq., xvi. 9) speaks of ' my bones,'' ' my body,^ he means, not his bones or his body only, but his soul, his self, and he has entirely forgotten the material meaning of the words which he is using.
Words for Soul in Sanskrit.
It is curious that the ancient lano-uao-e of India which generally allows us so clear an insight into the earliest history of words, should tell us so little by its names for soul or spirit.

The best known word for soul is atm an. But unfortunately its etymology is doubtful. It has been derived from AN, to breathe, a root which has yielded many words for wind, breath, soul, and mind. Thus we find in Sanskrit an-ila, wind, also an-ala, fire; in Greek av-ejjLo^, wind ; in Latin an-ima, air, wind, breath, but also life, soul, and even mind, and animus, soul and mind. But an -|- man would not give atm an, nor should it be forgotten that in the Veda we find tman also for atm an. Others have thought of the ness ; (4) n^lTT', and (5^ nTl, the two Inst being in the Bible epithets only of the soul. See an important article by Kohut, in Zeitschrift <f. D. M. G., xxi. p. 563. Kohut ascribes this fivefold division to Persian influences.

THE DISCOVERY OF THE SOUL. 201 root AV=VA, to blow, and have connected it with avTfxrjv. The original meaning, however, of atman was certainly breath. Thus the wind (vata) is called, Rv. V. 168, 4, atma devanam, the breath of the gods. In Rv. X. 16, 3, where the dead is addressed, we read: suryaTii kakshuh geikkhsitu vatam atma, let the eye go to the sun, the breath to the wind.

In other places atma can best be translated by life. To be separated from the atman (atmana viyukta) means to be dead. From meaning the vital principle, it came to be used for what we call the essence or substance. Thus in I. 164, 4, 'Who has seen him who was born first, when he who has no bones (form) bore him that has bones (form)? Where was the breath (asu), the blood (asW^), the essence (atma) of the world ? Who went to one who knew it, to ask him?' Here we see how atma is employed with the same metaphorical meaning as asu, breath, and Sisrig, blood. In another place, 1. 115, 1, the sun is called atma ^agataA tasthiisha^ k&., the soul of all that moves and stands. In later times atma meets us as the recognised name for soul, and the more abstract the conception of soul becomes, the more abstract the meaning conveyed by atma, till at last we cannot translate it any longer by soul, but must render it by self. Such has been the reaction of thought on language, that atman has become a recognised pronoun, corresponding to the Lat. ipse, the Greek avTo^. In some passages it can even be translated by body, as when we read in the Ka^Aa-upanishad IV. 12, the person (purusha), of the size of a thumb, stands in the midst of the atman, the body \ Having passed through ^ See also Taittiriya-upanishad I. 7 ', II. 1.
all these stages, atman was at last used by philosophers as the best name for the soul of the world, the Supreme Being.

While the derivation of atman is doubtful, it is clear that ana, breath, is derived from AN, to breathe, and likewise the more common praiia, breath. Pra^ia, however, does not only mean the breathing in its various modifications, or the life, but likewise what are called the five senses, viz. the nose, the tongue (speech), the eye, the ear, and the mind (manas), with their respective functions.

Other words for soul in Sanskrit are equally uninteresting. Pudgala means beautiful, then body, then soul, but why it has any of these meanings we cannot even guess. If we are told that k a also may be used for soul, this is evidently a merely philosophical name. It means AVho, and was used at a very early time as a name of the deity as well as of the soal.
Words for Soul in Tamil.

Before we proceed to examine the words for soul in Greek, we have still time to glance at some other languages in India, I mean the Dravidian. You know that, before the Aryas migrated into India, the country was occupied by people speaking a totally different language, a language which still lives all over the South of India, as Tamil, Telugu, Canarese, and in several local dialects.

In an able article on the study of Tamil, lately published in the Madras Christian College Magazine^ Dec, 1890, p. 1 5, the Kev. G. Mackenzie Cobban informs us that uyir is the Tamil word for life, and that where in English we should use the word soul, the Tamil man THE DISCOVEKY OF THE SOUL. 203 would use uyir. This uyir comes from a verb u-thu, which means to blow, or from u-yi, which means to breathe, and to exist. As, too, in Sanskrit, meant to breathe, though as a verb it means simply to he, as-mi, I am. But u-yir means more than breath and life. It means the soul. Another curious name for soul is kuttan, and this means a dancer, or a leaper, thus recalling the Greek Ovixos, which originally meant shaker or shaking or commotion, and the Gothic saivala, soul, which likewise seems to have had the original meaning of violent movement. There are other expressions for soul, or for mind, which simply mean what is within, like the Sk. anta/ikarajia, the working within.
Polynesian Words for Soul, &c.

We have only to consult the dictionaries of any language, whether spoken by civilised or uncivilised people, and we shall find everywhere the same process, that is to say, words meaning originally blood, heart, chest, breath, becoming in time the recognised terms for life, feeling, thinking, and soul. Mr. Edward Tregear has lately published a most excellent Comparative Dictionary of Maori and other Polynesian languages, particularly Samoan, Tahitian, Hawaian, Tongan, Rarotongan, Marquesan, Mangarevan, Paumotan, and Moriori. When we look for words for mind, we find, for instance, nianawa. This rnanawa means in Maori the belly, the heart, the lungs, breath, but also the seat of the afiections. In Samoan 7)idnaiva means to breathe, to rest ; Tiianatu, a thought. In Tahitian nianava is the stomach, the interior man, and manavanava is to think, to pon- 204 LECTURE VIT.

der ; onayiao, thought or idea ; dumanava, affection of the heai*t. In Hawaian mcmcnva stands for affection, but likewise for spirit, and even for a spirit, as an apparition; mana is intelligence, r)ianao to think, 7)iananao, thought, opinion.

In Tongan onanava is breath, life ; to throb, to pulsate, and also to be careful of In Mangaian 7)ianava is mind, spirit ; in Marquesan vienava is breath, life, soul ; in Mangarevan soul and conscience.

Another word for bowels and lieart in Maori is ngakau, but it is also used for heart as the seat of the affections and of sorrow. In Tahitian, the same word appears regularly as aau, and means heart, mind, courage, spirit, and conscience. In Hawaian naau still signifies the small intestines of men and animals, but it is likewise used for the seat of the affections and of thought, for memory, conscience, learning, and wisdom.

A third word, hinengaro, is used in Maori for a certain portion of the intestines, but likewise for heart and affections ; in Samoan for will and desire ; in Tahitian for love, desire, will, choice, pleasure ; in Tongan, as finengcdo, for the mind, but applied to a king only.
Material Beg'inning's.

We have learnt so far that the discovery of the soul, the first attempts at naming the soul, started everywhere from the simplest observations of material facts. It was the running away of the blood, the beating of the heart, the breathing, and more particularly the cessation of breathing at the time of death, which THE DISCOVERY OF THE SOUL. 205 suggested the idea that there was something different from the decaying body, and at the same time supplied the first names for that something. In fact the lesson cannot be inculcated too often that the whole wealth of our most abstract and spiritual words comes from a small number of material or concrete terms.
zh: 在希伯來文中，表示「靈魂」的詞主要有三個：*Nephesh*（נֶפֶשׁ）、*Ruach*（רוּחַ）與*Neshamah*（נְשָׁמָה）。這三個詞各自承載不同的語意層次，正如在其他語言中，靈魂的概念往往與呼吸、氣息或生命原則相關。*Nephesh* 通常指涉活物的生命、慾望或個體自身，近似於希臘文的 *ψυχή*（psyche）；*Ruach* 則意指風、氣息或精神，與希臘文的 *πνεῦμα*（pneuma）相對應；而 *Neshamah* 更側重於來自上帝的氣息，賦予人生命與理智。這些詞彙在《聖經》文本中交互使用，反映出希伯來思想對靈魂的理解並非單一實體，而是多面向的動態概念。
讓我們來看一些希伯來文中關於靈魂的詞語。身體與賦予其生命的某物之間的區分，對猶太人來說從最早的時代就已為人所知^。身體是肉體，ib'Zi、basar。因此「所有的肉體」意指全人類，每一個活著的生物，而「我的肉體」被用來表示我自己。肉體，作為活躍的和有生命的，一方面與無生命的東西——石頭或骨頭相對；另一方面，由於它是凡滅的、虛弱的、會衰亡的，它被區別於永恆的、不朽的東西，即神與靈魂。
接著產生了一個問題：猶太人如何稱呼那不是身體的東西？他們用不同的名稱來稱呼它，而這些名稱起初具有非常模糊和未經界定的含義，以至於它們常被互相替換使用。若要嘗試界定^ 參見 H. Schultz, Alttestamentliche Theologie, p. 622.
嚴格來說，這些詞彙的使用是一個錯誤，儘管在後來的時代，猶太人本身曾試圖為他們古老而模糊的用語下定義。
其中一個詞是 *nəšî, riiach*，其原義是指「運動者」，可指風或呼吸。既然雷鳴被稱為上帝的聲音，暴風雨便被理解為祂的呼吸。人身上的這個 *riiach* 是生命，由上帝所賦予，亦將歸返於上帝。它用來指人的呼吸，但不僅限於呼吸，同樣也表示我們所謂的靈魂，因為我們讀到 *riiach* 被激怒、被壓低、被悲傷籠罩，或被復甦而喜悅。
另一個詞 נְשָׁמָה（neshamah）的意義幾乎相同。它用來指稱每一個受造物從上帝所領受的生命之氣，同樣也用來指稱呼吸並生活的受造物本身。因此，當我們在《創世記》第二章第七節讀到「耶和華上帝用地上的塵土造人，將生氣吹在他鼻孔裡，他就成了有靈的活人」時，neshamah 這個詞顯然被理解為來自上帝的氣息，它不僅賦予生命，也賦予我們所謂的靈魂，後者若無前者則不可能存在。

第三個詞彙 K'sj（nephesh）從一開始似乎也沒有表達過非常不同的意涵。但它很快就開始被用來指稱個人的靈魂，而非籠統地指生命與精神。Nephesh 是活著的靈魂，是自我意識的靈魂。因此正是 nephesh 而非他物會愛與恨、喜與悲，而「我的 nephesh」是一個非常普遍的表達方式，用以表示我本身。我們遇見諸如「我的靈魂活著」、「我的靈魂死去」等表達，甚至死者仍被稱為一個 nephesh。但要在 nephesh 出現的每一處經文裡判斷究竟是意指呼吸、生命或靈魂，實乃不可能。當以利亞為死去的孩童祈禱時，他說：「耶和華我的上帝啊，我懇求你，讓這孩童的靈魂回到他的內裡。」「耶和華垂聽以利亞的聲音，孩童的靈魂回到他身上，他就活了。」在此處（列王紀上十七章二十一節），靈魂可能僅指呼吸、實際的呼吸，如同我們說一位溺水者已藉由向其肺部吹氣而被救活一樣。但它也可能意指由上帝所賦予、在死亡時回歸上帝的靈魂。
我們已經看到，這個靈魂有時被說成是在血液中，或就是血液本身。就像血液一樣，心臟（希伯來文：leb）也被設想為靈魂的座位，或被同義地用來指代靈魂。不僅感情與熱情，而且良心，乃至理性與悟性，都住在心臟中。因此我們讀到純淨的心（a pure heart）、新的心（a new heart）。我們在《何西阿書》第七章第十一節讀到以法蓮「如同無心的愚鴿」，即缺乏悟性。
我們在此看到古代語言中普遍存在的那種表達財富。同一事物根據打動說話者的某些特徵標記，而被稱以不同的名稱。這些名稱起初幾乎是同義的，逐漸通過使用而分化，直到一個時刻到來，它們才被文法學家、哲學家或神學家或多或少地武斷地界定^。
在後來的時代，猶太教拉比們對靈魂的各種名稱定義得更加精確，但也更加武斷。他們告訴我們（《創世紀密德拉什》[Midrash Genes. Rabba, cap. 14 ad fin.]）人身中有五種精神潛能，各各具有自己的名稱。它們是：(1) נפש（nefesh），生命力；(2) רוח（ruach），生命之靈；(3) נשמה（neshamah），意識……200　第七講

對我們來說，重要的是觀察到一個事實：所有這些詞彙的原始意義都是物質性的。生命的觀念是從血液、心臟、呼吸中借用而來，而該觀念隨著時間的推移，不僅包含了生理上的生命，還包含了感受、感官知覺與理解，乃至靈魂本身——作為一個自我意識的、人格的，即使並非永恆的存在。希伯來文中還有其他表達方式，其起點更加物質化，卻最終意指靈魂或自我。因此，當詩人（第六篇第3節起，第十六篇第9節）說「我的骨頭」、「我的身體」時，他所指的不僅僅是他的骨頭或身體，而是他的靈魂、他的自我，他已經完全忘卻了所使用詞彙的物質意義。
梵文中表示靈魂的詞彙
奇特的是，印度古代語言通常能使我們清晰洞察詞彙的最早歷史，卻對靈魂或精神的名稱告訴我們甚少。

靈魂最廣為人知的詞彙是 atman。但不幸的是，其詞源學頗成疑問。有人將其追溯至 AN，即呼吸之意，此根衍生出許多關於風、呼吸、靈魂和心思的詞彙。因此我們在梵文中發現 an-ila（風）、an-ala（火）；在希臘文中發現 aνεμος（風）；在拉丁文中發現 anima（空氣、風、呼吸，亦指生命、靈魂，甚至心思）和 animus（靈魂和心思）。但是 an + man 並不能產生 atman，且應當注意，在《吠陀》中我們也發現 tman 作為 atman 的用法。其他學者則轉向考察閃語族的詞彙，並指出希伯來文中存在：(1) נפש（nefesh）；(2) רוח（ruach）；(3) חיה（haya）；(4) יצר（yetzer）；及 (5) לב（leb），後兩者在《聖經》中僅作為靈魂的修飾詞。詳見 Kohut 在《德國東方學會期刊》(Zeitschrift für Deutsche Morgenländische Gesellschaft) 第二十一卷第五百六十三頁所發表的重要論文。Kohut 將此五分法歸因於波斯的影響。
根 AV=VA 表示「吹」之意，有人將其與 ἀνέμων 相連結。不過，atman 的原始意義確實是「呼吸」。因此風（vata）在《梨俱吠陀》第五卷 168 篇 4 頌被稱為 atma devanam，即「諸神的呼吸」。在《梨俱吠陀》第十卷 16 篇 3 頌中，對亡者言及之處，我們讀到：suryam kakshuh geikkhsitu vatam atma，「令眼睛歸於太陽，呼吸歸於風」。

在其他地方，atma 最好譯為「生命」。與 atman（atmana viyukta）分離意味著死亡。從表示生命原則的含義，它後來被用於我們所謂的本質或實體。因此在《梨俱吠陀》I. 164, 4 中，「誰見過首先被生出者，當那無骨骼（無形）者生出有骨骼（有形）者時？呼吸（asu）、血液（asṛ）、世界的本質（atma）在何處？誰去到知道它的人那裡，向他提問？」在此我們看到 atma 如何與 asu（呼吸）和 asṛ（血液）採用相同的隱喻含義。在另一處，I. 115, 1 中，太陽被稱為 atma jagatas tasthushyaś ca，即一切流動和靜止者的靈魂。在後來的時代，atma 作為靈魂的公認名稱出現，而且靈魂的概念愈來愈抽象，atma 所傳達的含義也愈來愈抽象，最終我們不再能以「靈魂」來譯它，而必須將其譯為「自我」。思想對語言的反作用已達到如此程度，atman 成為公認的代詞，對應拉丁文的 ipse 和希臘文的 autos。在某些段落中，它甚至可以譯為「身體」，如我們在《卡塔奧義書》IV. 12 中所讀到的，那拇指大小的人格（purusha）站立於 atman、身體的中央。經過⁷ --- ⁷ 另見《泰蒂利耶奧義書》I. 7；II. 1。
在所有這些階段中，哲學家終於將「阿特曼」（atman）用作為世界之魂、至高存在的最佳稱號。
雖然「atman」(阿特曼)的詞源不確定，但很明顯「ana」(呼吸)乃衍生自 AN(呼吸之意)，同樣地更常見的「prana」(生命力)亦然。然而「prana」(生命力)不僅意指呼吸在其各種變化中的形式，或生命本身，同時也指所謂的五感，即鼻、舌(語言)、眼、耳及心智(manas)，及其各自之功能。
梵文中的其他靈魂詞彙同樣乏善可陳。Pudgala 本意為美麗，後來用來指身體，再後來指靈魂，但我們無法推測它為何具有這些含義。如果有人告訴我們 ka 也可用來表示靈魂，這顯然只是一個純粹的哲學用語。它的意思是「誰」（ka），在很早的時代就被用來作為神祇和靈魂的名稱。

In Tamil, a language belonging to the Turanian family, we find the word *kili*, which means bird, used also to denote the soul. This is extremely interesting, because it shows how the same metaphor, which we found in the Rig-Veda, and which led to the doctrine of the transmigration of souls, was current among the Dravidian races. The word *uyir*, which in Tamil means life or soul, is probably connected with the root *vai*, to swell or grow, and thus originally meant something that swells or grows, or is alive. 在屬於圖蘭語族的泰米爾語中，我們發現詞彙 *kili*（意為鳥）也被用來指代靈魂。這極其有趣，因為它表明我們在梨俱吠陀中發現的同一隱喻——導致靈魂輪迴說的隱喻——在達羅毗荼種族中同樣流行。泰米爾語中表示生命或靈魂的詞彙 *uyir*，可能與詞根 *vai*（膨脹或生長）相關，因此最初指的是某種膨脹或生長的東西，或者說是活著的東西。
在我們着手檢驗希臘文中關於靈魂的詞彙之前，我們仍有時間瞥一眼印度的某些其他語言，我是指達羅毗荼語族。諸位知道，在雅利安人遷入印度之前，該國由說著完全不同語言的民族所佔據，這種語言至今仍活躍於印度南方各地，如泰米爾語、特魯古語、卡納達語，以及若干地方方言。

在《馬德拉斯基督教學院雜誌》（*Madras Christian College Magazine*）一八九○年十二月號第十五頁上發表的一篇關於泰米爾語研究的傑出文章中，牧師G‧麥肯齊‧科班（Rev. G. Mackenzie Cobban）告訴我們，*uyir* 是泰米爾語中表示生命的詞彙，而在英語中我們應使用「靈魂」（soul）這個詞的地方，泰米爾人會使用 *uyir*。這個 *uyir* 來自動詞 *u-thu*（意為吹氣），或來自 *u-yi*（意為呼吸和存在）。同樣地，梵文的 *as* 意為呼吸，雖然作為動詞它單純意為存在，如 *as-mi*，我存在。然而 *u-yir* 的含義不止於呼吸和生命，它意為靈魂。另一個關於靈魂的奇特名稱是 *kuttan*，意為舞者或跳躍者，由此令人想起希臘文的 *σεισμός* （Seismos），原本意為震動者或震動或騷動，以及哥德語的 *saivala*（靈魂），它似乎也具有激烈運動的原始含義。還有其他表達靈魂或心智的表達方式，其含義單純指內在的東西，如梵文的 *antah-karana*（內部運作）。
波利尼西亞語中關於靈魂等詞彙

我們只需查閱任何語言的字典，無論該語言是由開化或未開化的民族所使用，我們就會發現同樣的過程無處不在，也就是說，原本意為血液、心臟、胸部、呼吸的詞彙，久而久之便成為公認用來表示生命、感受、思想和靈魂的術語。愛德華‧特雷吉爾先生最近出版了一部極其優秀的《毛利語及其他波利尼西亞語言比較字典》，特別涵蓋薩摩亞語、塔希提語、夏威夷語、湯加語、拉羅通加語、馬克薩斯語、曼加雷瓦語、帕烏莫圖語及莫里奧里語。當我們尋找表示心智的詞彙時，例如發現 nianawa 一詞。這個 nianawa 在毛利語中意為腹部、心臟、肺部、呼吸，但也指感受的所在。在薩摩亞語中 nianava 意為呼吸、休息；nianatu 則表示一個思想。在塔希提語中 nianava 是胃、內在的人，而 manavanava 則是思考、思索的意思。
在夏威夷語中，moemoemana 代表情感，但同樣也代表精神，甚至代表精神作為顯現；mana 是智慧，manao 是思考，mananao 是思想、意見。
在湯加語中，onanava 是呼吸、生命；搏動、脈動，亦表示小心謹慎。在曼蓋亞語中，ianava 是心智、精神；在馬克薩斯語中，vienava 是呼吸、生命、靈魂；在曼蓋雷瓦語中是靈魂與良心。
毛利語中，「腸臟」和「心臟」的另一個詞是 ngakau，但它也用於「心臟」，作為感情和悲傷的所在。在大溪地語中，同一個詞規律地出現為 aau，意為心臟、心智、勇氣、精神和良心。在夏威夷語中，naau 仍然表示人和動物的小腸，但它同樣被用於感情和思想的所在，以及記憶、良心、學習和智慧。
第三個詞是 hinengaro，在毛利語中用來指稱腸道的某個部分，但同樣也表示心臟與感情；在薩摩亞語中表示意志與慾望；在大溪地語中表示愛、慾望、意志、抉擇、快樂；在湯加語中，作為 finengcdo，表示心智，但僅適用於國王。
物質的起源
我們迄今已經瞭解到，靈魂的發現、給靈魂命名的最初嘗試，到處都是從對物質事實的最簡單觀察開始的。正是血液的流逝、心臟的跳動、呼吸，尤其是死亡時刻呼吸的停止，激發了這樣的觀念：存在著某種有別於腐朽軀體的東西，同時也為那某種東西提供了最初的名稱。事實上，這個教訓再怎麼強調也不為過——我們最抽象且最精神性的詞彙的全部財富，都源自於少數幾個物質性或具體性的術語。
